note:
自从1996年春天，24岁的我在“偏执”杂志上发表了我的第一篇文章以来，我已经沉浸在阴谋论的狂野世界里24年了。“偏执狂”给我留下最深刻印象的是其页面中可用信息的无政府状态。它不是一本右翼阴谋杂志。它不是一本左翼阴谋杂志。它甚至不存在于这两个极点之间。它的编辑使命(或非使命)受制于远远超出这些限制参数的价值观(或非价值观)，致力于从多个角度对边缘信仰的极端进行分类和分析。正如马歇尔·麦克卢汉(Marshall McLuhan)曾经说过的那样，当观点取代洞察力和理解力时，它可能是一种危险的奢侈品。“偏执狂”的编辑们致力于不发表观点。这与宣传完全相反。就其本质而言，宣传排除了任何与专职宣传者意图传播的信息相矛盾或破坏的信息。
阴谋论一直被我们现在称为说服工程师的人用作大规模灌输的工具。一种看似合理且足够可怕的好阴谋论，可能比一千次有理有据的政治演说更有价值。
在我的第一本书“隐蔽目录学：作为艺术形式的阴谋论”中，我将阴谋论分为五个不同的类别：1)疯狂，2)虚假信息，3)错误信息，4)讽刺，5)合法研究。有些理论设法将两个或多个类别合并为一个类别。只有在非常罕见的情况下，这样的理论才能将所有这五个类别结合在一起。这种混合形式的阴谋论最近一次--也可以说是最有影响力的--最早出现在2017年，由一张匿名的4chan海报公布，只被称为&34；Q&34；或&34；Qanon。你最近很可能听说了很多关于这件事的事情。至少有一名QAnon的支持者几乎肯定会在11月当选国会议员，这一特殊阴谋论的信徒热切支持唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)连任。
普通人在过去几十年里没有研究过阴谋论的起源(我可以补充一句，这是一个合理的选择)，他们可能不会认识到构成错综复杂的QAnon叙事的大多数准超现实元素的起源。
今年早些时候，也就是3月份，我和一位朋友谈论新冠肺炎和国家禁闭。他比我大10岁，住在中西部的一个小镇上。我住在加利福尼亚州的长滩。在电话里和他聊着网上教我的英语课出现的所有意想不到的困难时，他突然主动提出意见，认为新冠肺炎在2020年会是一个积极的发展。
他接着真诚地告诉我，在特朗普2020年再次当选之后，他将为美国人民提供免费能源。不仅如此，他还将取消所得税。现在，就在此时此刻，美国军队已经被部署到地下，他们正忙于清理地下秘密隧道，从撒旦奴隶手中救出数百名儿童，并清除黑帽。我的朋友当时毫不犹豫地坚称，这一改变游戏规则的消息很快就会传出。
这是一件很棒的事，他用有分寸的语气告诉我。特朗普将不得不使用紧急广播系统将这条新闻传递给美国人民，因为媒体一直在撒谎，Twitter和YouTube等社交媒体正在审查和删除以真实方式报道现实的视频。
此外，我的朋友以绝对肯定的语气表示，特朗普的幕后支持者(我的朋友称之为白帽)最近从魔鬼崇拜者手中夺取了整个谷歌公司的控制权，这就是为什么你现在可以从那个特定的搜索引擎检索到准确的信息。
他似乎不愿意告诉我。起初他支支吾吾，然后喃喃地说：噢，…。就这些留言板。&#34；
&#34；Well…。什么留言板？他不愿说。你能把链接发给我吗？我问。
当我进一步询问有关这方面的更多细节时，他阴谋诡计地低声低语，要求我在谷歌上搜索肾上腺皮质激素这个词。
我第一次读亨特·S·汤普森(Hunter S.Thompson)的经典纪实作品“拉斯维加斯的恐惧与厌恶”是在我18岁的时候。从2002年开始，我就断断续续地在加州州立大学长滩分校(Cal State Long Beach)的英语作文课上教授这本书。我很清楚汤普森的另一个自我，拉乌尔·杜克，当冈佐博士(杜克的律师)建议他服用这种药物时，他遇到了肾上腺素，因为它让纯粹的麦斯卡林看起来像姜汁啤酒。他还警告杜克，如果你服用太多，你会完全发疯的。杜克声称肾上腺素的致幻作用。(注：汤普森的另一个自我--拉乌尔·杜克是他的律师)，他建议他服用这种药物是因为它让纯美斯卡林看起来像姜汁啤酒。杜克还警告杜克，如果你服用太多，你会完全发疯的。杜克声称，肾上腺素的致幻作用。
即使在年仅18岁的时候，我就知道这个有灵感的想法是汤普森在其他新闻叙事中众多变幻莫测的副产品之一。汤普森以绕道进入门肖森级别的胡言乱语而闻名，比如有一次，他声称参议员埃德·马斯基(Ed Muskie)在1972年总统竞选期间大剂量服用一种名为伊博加因(Ibogaine)的迷幻药物，这是汤普森在竞选之路上讲述的一个滑稽(但完全是虚假的)故事。
在2003年特里·乔里安(Terry Gilliam)改编自电影“拉斯维加斯的恐惧与厌恶”(Fear And Abathing)的2003年Critarion DVD发行的评论中，乔里安证实，肾上腺素是亨特编造的一种完全虚构的胡说八道的药物。紧接着，乔里安发表了一句诡异的先见之明，几乎预示着这种药物将被引入QAnon不断扩大的神话中：{#34；{##}{##**$$}。我有肾上腺皮质激素！&他们在谈论从哪里买到的。哦，有个家伙能帮我弄到肾上腺素。我就是喜欢这个大谎言的一贯作风。它对希特勒管用，对我们这样的人(即艺术家和讲故事的人)也管用。
这似乎也适用于Qanon，因为它完全是编造出来的胡说八道，会在容易上当受骗的人、半知情的人、沮丧的人和精神错乱的人心目中成为事实。
所以我完全按照朋友的建议去做了。我在谷歌上搜索了肾上腺色素这个词，记住我的朋友坚持认为，支持特朗普的白帽已经控制了谷歌，所以当我输入这个特定的词时，我只能从那个搜索引擎收到准确的信息。
第一个出现的结果是艾米丽在2020年3月17日发表在衍生产品“与新冠肺炎阴谋论者一起钻进兔子洞”的文章，开篇如下：
今天，我掉进了一群狂热的特朗普支持者的兔子洞里，他们相信新冠肺炎既是一个恶作剧，也是特朗普作为总统最伟大的时刻。他显然行动果断，同时也知道新冠肺炎是好莱坞自由派精英掩盖…。。肾上腺色素A是一种针对好莱坞自由派精英的药物，由含有肾上腺激素的实际人类脑干制成。希拉里·克林顿通过在披萨店…折磨儿童来制造这种药物。。汤姆·汉克斯对肾上腺素上瘾，他从撒旦教会的高级女祭司席琳·迪翁身上拿到了最新一批受污染的肾上腺素，并抓住了新冠肺炎。她精通毒药，因为她一直在孩子们的服装系列中添加一种让我们的孩子保持中性的化学物质。汤姆·汉克斯(Tom Hanks)在金球奖获奖感言中向好莱坞自由派精英阴谋深州发出信号，表示肾上腺素将会短缺。艾伦已经关闭了她的录音室观众，因为她也上瘾了。
读完这篇文章的全文后，我给我的朋友发了一封电子邮件，指出当我用谷歌搜索他的建议并在谷歌上搜索时，第一篇弹出的文章讽刺地将整个阴谋论撕得粉碎。这与支持特朗普的白帽们完全控制谷歌的想法有什么不同？
我没有收到对这个问题的直接回答。取而代之的是，我被告知搜索瑞秋·钱德勒(Rachel Chandler)的名字。这会以某种方式回答我所有的问题。绕过谷歌那些烦人的白帽子，我决定使用另一个名为Million Short的搜索引擎，几乎立刻就找到了2019年3月22日“每日点评”(Daily Dot)的一篇文章，题为“QAnon正在以一种令人不安和危险的方式攻击一个随机女人”，其中详细描述了QAnon的帖子是如何奇怪地集中在一位名叫雷切尔·钱德勒(Rachel Chandler)的摄影师兼选角导演身上的，她是拥有报纸的钱德勒家族的一员，但没有明显的政治足迹，也没有明显的政治足迹。
Q发布了似乎没完没了的帖子提到钱德勒，暗示她在2017年拍了一张比尔·克林顿和登记在册的儿童性犯罪者杰弗里·爱泼斯坦(Jeffrey Epstein)在游泳池里一起玩耍的照片(爱泼斯坦被特朗普现任内阁官员亚历山大·阿科斯塔(Alexander Acosta)判处轻刑，目前正在接受调查)。
除此之外，QAnon指控钱德勒为爱泼斯坦招揽女孩，让她的摄影工作室得到精英撒旦教徒的资助，这些人用它来举行仪式，用她的照片炫耀爱泼斯坦性和酷刑房间的受害者，这与西好莱坞…标准酒店发生的一起至今不明原因(但据说很可怕)的事件有关联。该事件至今仍未得到解释(但被认为是可怕的)，她的摄影工作室由精英撒旦教徒资助，用来炫耀爱泼斯坦房间里的性受害者和酷刑受害者，这与西好莱坞标准酒店发生的一起至今仍未解释(但据称是可怕的)事件有关联。。
需要澄清的是，钱德勒没有被任何执法机构…指控有任何不当行为。。Qanon声称她拍了一张台球照，证明她与爱泼斯坦有关，但没有证据表明她与爱泼斯坦有关，照片上甚至没有爱泼斯坦的照片-尽管照片上有乔治·纳德，这位被判有恋童癖的人在穆勒调查中提供了关于唐纳德·特朗普总统与俄罗斯官员会面的证据。
然而，钱德勒的名字已经完全卷入了这场阴谋，以至于谷歌搜索的首页只显示了与Qanon相关的内容，而在搜索她的名字时出现的前六个视频中，有五个是Qanon的追随者。
Qanon和他的众多支持者发布的许多视频和猜测都没有提供钱德勒和杰弗里·爱泼斯坦之间任何明确的联系，但不知何故，这条转移视线的话题受到了数千名业余侦探的密切关注，他们中的大多数人无疑都真诚地渴望了解真相。
有人可能会说，正如艾米丽在她的上述衍生文章中所写的那样，当人们感觉自己无法控制自己的生活时--比如在全球大流行期间--阴谋论令人欣慰的确定性似乎越来越有吸引力。有人可能还会辩称，种族主义、仇外心理和(或许最致命的)新恐惧症助长了人们对这类理论的兴趣。但我认为，这些解释虽然部分有道理，但忽略了此时此刻困扰美国的真正问题的核心。
真正的问题是，真正的阴谋每天都在我们眼前展开，但当主流媒体出于各种原因(主要原因是重写公司新闻稿并在最终产品上贴上一个署名)而避免报道此类阴谋时，比起真正拿自己的声誉冒险来处理过多令人不快的真相，普通人有必要自己填补空白。这些人可能无知，但他们并不愚蠢。他们本能地知道，他们每天都是公然谎言的牺牲品。因此，他们在没有任何培训的情况下，通过搜索4chan、8chan、8kun和Reddit等众多可靠的在线资源，自己进行研究。
[W]为什么这么容易找到想要相信阴谋的人[？]。我的回答是：因为给这么多人带来创伤的很多事情都是阴谋。
阿片类药物的流行是像萨克勒夫妇这样的富裕家庭和轮流进出行业的监管机构之间的阴谋。波音737危机是由波音合谋偷工减料和航空监管机构合谋允许航空公司自我监管造成的。
参议员们合谋在冠状病毒封锁前结清头寸，富有的跨国公司合谋获得小企业PPP基金94.5%的股份，大型科技公司合谋通过非法串通操纵工资，而快餐特许经营公司对竞业禁止采取同样的做法。
在一个不断发生摧毁生命和地球的真实阴谋丑闻的世界里，阴谋论具有真正的解释力。
多克托罗在这里说的一切都是真的；然而，我们很少将这样的临床观察应用到我们个人认识的人身上。通常，我们会用这样的解释来驳斥网上遇到的奇怪陌生人的歪曲信仰。在今年3月之前，我知道我的朋友是一个理性的人，有相当多的现实世界经验和常识，而不是一个容易被不合逻辑的言辞或夸张的胡言乱语所左右的人。另一方面，自从上一次总统选举和2017年11月从互联网默默无闻中脱颖而出的Qanon出生以来，我就没有与他保持过密切联系。巧合的是，这也是特朗普支持率跌至接近历史低点的同一时期。(Qanon自2017年11月以来对特朗普支持率的全面影响还没有任何实质性的研究。)
大约四周过去了，我给我的朋友发了一封电子邮件，又问了他几个关于QAnon的问题，试图理解为什么有人会相信这么多未经证实的说法，而几乎没有人持怀疑态度。当然，我想，肯定有一些至少有一半合理的东西来支持这些离谱的说法。我需要知道消息来源，这样我才能自己评估索赔。5月3日，我给他发了以下电子邮件：
我正试图理清这整件事，所以也许你能帮我。我有几个问题。
为什么美国军方要清除秘密的地下基地，而这些基地最初是由美国军方建造的呢？除了美国军方，还有谁会负责在美国领土上建造的大型地下基地？
特朗普为什么会对实施自由能源感兴趣？他不是想重振煤炭工业吗？在我看来，自由能源将损害他所有的商业利益。
根据最可靠的消息来源，谷歌上的审查目前似乎还很活跃，这并不是什么新鲜事。(根据我的经验，我发现Million Short.com是一个更好的搜索引擎工具。)。
我不能肯定在我们当前的政治形势下是否存在白帽子。也许黑帽、黑帽和灰帽会不会是更准确的说法呢？“黑帽”和“灰帽”会不会是更准确的说法呢？
从活人身上收获的肾上腺色素的整个概念不是起源于亨特·S·汤普森(Hunter S.Thompson)--作为一个讽刺概念吗？我一直以为“拉斯维加斯的恐惧与厌恶”中的那段话是个玩笑。Qanon是否声称亨特·S·汤普森(Hunter S.Thompson)在写这本书时已经掌握了内幕消息？
第二天，我的朋友用另一个问题回答了我的问题：你是一名研究人员。你为什么要玩DUM.B.？就是这样。没别的了。
因为我不知道他为什么要把这个词写成首字母缩写，所以我回答说，我不是在玩DUMB，老实说，我不知道这些问题的答案。我正在试着理解提议的情景。如果你有答案，我很乐意听听。
那天晚些时候，他给我回了信，解释说D.U.M.B.代表的是深层地下军事基地。这一解释使第一条信息比以前更令人困惑。(我怎么可能玩地下深层军事基地呢？我克制住要求他进一步澄清的冲动，因为我怀疑他的回答只会增加我的困惑。)。他还给我发了几个链接，尽管一个月前他坚持说他不可能给我发送任何链接。他从来没有费心解释这种情况在过去四周里是如何或为什么发生了变化。
不可避免的是，第一个链接将我引向了Qanon的帖子。这些帖子中的大多数都非常隐晦，没有回答我的任何问题。
在回答我关于特朗普向美国人民提供免费能源的问题时，我的朋友回答说，美国联邦调查局(FBI)要求特朗普总统的叔叔约翰调查尼古拉·特斯拉(Nikola Tesla)的文件。这句简短的话之后是一个链接，指向2017年8月2日Heavy.com上一篇题为“约翰·特朗普，唐纳德的叔叔：你需要知道的5个快速事实”(John Trump，Donald‘s Uncle：5 Fast Fact You Need Know)的帖子。我的朋友在链接之后写道：你想知道我将来在哪里为这个国家提供免费能源。
Heavy.com的帖子中没有任何内容表明唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)在他的第二个任期内致力于向美国提供免费能源。
他试图回答我的另一个问题，他写道，如果谷歌的审查制度是开启的，那么为什么当你搜索瑞秋·钱德勒(Rachel Chandler)时，你会得到所有关于她的信息？
当然，这是一个循环推理的完美例子。QAnon说的关于瑞秋·钱德勒的一切都是真的，因为当你在谷歌上搜索瑞秋·钱德勒时，QAnon的所有帖子都会弹出。因此，卡农说的关于瑞秋·钱德勒的一切都是真的。(如果是A，那么是B。如果是B，那么是A。或者也许我应该说，如果是Q，那么就是Q。如果是Q，那么就是Q。)。
为了回答我的其他问题，他给我发了三个YouTube视频的链接，其中一个是关于地下深处军事基地的不为人知的故事。
第一个视频(标题为“地下战争，正在发生”)展示了一位名叫里克的基督教爱国者与一位自称“基因”的匿名人士的对话，这些谣言时长38分16秒，讲的是美国基督教士兵在地下深处与崇拜恶魔的光明会成员作战的故事，这些谣言都是毫无事实根据的，是关于美国基督教徒士兵在地下深处与崇拜恶魔的光明会成员作战的，我听了38分16秒的未经证实的谣言，这些谣言都是关于美国基督教士兵在地下深处与崇拜恶魔的光明会成员作战的，当时他正在与一位自称“基因”的匿名人士交谈，这些谣言时长38分16秒。
这将我引向了一个更复杂的YouTube视频，名为“2胜1负-地下战争细节！”第八部分-基因解码-B2T秀，在其中我了解到吉恩决定冒着生命危险将这些危险的秘密公之于众，因为在决定性的一天，上帝联系了吉恩，并明确告诉他，这些信息必须公开！在这一点上，吉恩与一位匿名的前加拿大军官联手揭露了关于这些地下军事基地深处的令人不安的真相(这是一个。动物和人类的祭祀滋养着生物的内脏--换句话说，我们谈论的是远在人类到来之前就已经在地球上栖息的恶魔般的可怕的东西。
在这一令人震惊的发现之后，吉恩进一步阐述了这一情景：新的世界秩序正处于最后阶段-也就是说，在特朗普出现之前，它们是最后阶段-最后阶段。
.
note_en:
I've been involved in the wild world of conspiracy theories for 24 years now, ever since I published my first article in the pages of Paranoia Magazine in the spring of 1996 when I was 24 years old. What most impressed me about Paranoia was the anarchy of information available within its pages. It wasn't a right-wing conspiracy magazine. It wasn't a left-wing conspiracy magazine. It didn't even exist   between  these two poles. Its editorial mission (or non-mission) was beholden to values (or non-values) that lay far beyond these limiting parameters, a dedication to cataloging and analyzing the extremes of fringe beliefs from multiple points of view. As Marshall McLuhan once said,  "A point of view can be a dangerous luxury when substituted for insight and understanding. "  The editors of Paranoia dedicated themselves to    not  having a point of view. It was the exact opposite of propaganda. By its very nature, propaganda excludes any information that contradicts or undermines the message the dedicated propagandist is intent on disseminating.
 Conspiracy theories have always been used by what we now call "persuasion engineers" as tools of mass indoctrination. A good conspiracy theory that seems plausible and frightening enough can be worth more than a thousand well-reasoned stump speeches.
  In my first book, "    Cryptoscatology: Conspiracy Theory as Art Form ," I broke conspiracy theories down into five distinct categories: 1) Insanity, 2) Disinformation, 3) Misinformation, 4) Satire, and 5) Legitimate Research. Some theories manage to merge two or more categories into one. Only on very rare occasions do such theories manage to combine    all  five categories. The most recent — and arguably most impactful — strain of this hybrid form of conspiracy-mongering first emerged in 2017, promulgated by an anonymous 4chan poster known only as "Q" — or "QAnon." You've been  hearing a lot about this lately, most likely. At least one QAnon supporter  is almost certain to be elected to Congress in November, and devotees of this particular conspiracy theory are eagerly supporting the re-election of Donald Trump.
 The average person, who has    not  spent the past couple of decades studying the origins of conspiracy theories (a reasonable choice, I might add), would probably not recognize the origins of most of the quasi-surreal elements that make up the convoluted QAnon narrative.
 Earlier this year, in March, I was talking to a friend about COVID-19 and the national lockdown. He's 10 years older than me and lives in a small town in the Midwest. I live in Long Beach, California. While chatting with him on the phone about all the unexpected difficulties that have arisen from teaching my English classes online, he suddenly volunteered the opinion that COVID-19 would be a    positive  development in 2020.
   He proceeded to tell me, with complete sincerity, that after Trump is re-elected in 2020, he will deliver "free energy" to the people of America. Not only that, he's also going to abolish the income tax. Right now,   at this very moment , United States troops have been deployed underground where they're busy "cleaning out" covert subterranean tunnels, "saving hundreds of children from satanic slaves," and kicking out the "black hats." Without skipping a beat, my friend then insisted that news of this game-changing development would be "coming out" soon.
 "It's a great thing," he told me in measured tones. "Trump will have to use the Emergency Broadcast System to give this news to the American people because the media keeps lying and social media like Twitter and YouTube are censoring and deleting videos that report reality the way it actually is."
  Furthermore, my friend said in tones of absolute certainty, Trump supporters working behind the scenes (referred to by my friend as the "white hats") had recently wrested control of the entire Google corporation from devil worshippers, which is why you could now retrieve "accurate information" from that particular search engine.
   He seemed reluctant to tell me. At first he hemmed and hawed, then muttered, "Oh … just these message boards."
 "Well … what message boards?" He wouldn't say. "Could you send me the links?" I asked.
     When I pushed further and asked for more details about this "accurate information," in a conspiratorial whisper he urged me to search the word "Adrenochrome" in Google.
  I first read Hunter S. Thompson's classic nonfiction book, "Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas" when I was 18. I've taught the book in my English composition classes at Cal State Long Beach, off and on, since 2002. I'm well aware that Thompson's alter ego, Raoul Duke, encounters Adrenochrome when Dr. Gonzo (Duke's speed-fueled attorney) suggests that he ingest the drug because it "makes pure mescaline seem like ginger beer." He also warns Duke, "You'll go completely crazy if you take too much."  Duke claims that the hallucinogenic effects of Adrenochrome work best when the substance is harvested from the adrenaline glands of living human bodies.
  Even at the tender age of 18, I knew that this inspired idea was one of Thompson's many phantasmagorical asides in an otherwise journalistic narrative. Thompson was famous for these detours into Munchausen-level jabberwocky, like the time he claimed that Sen. Ed Muskie was taking "massive doses" of a psychedelic drug called Ibogaine during the 1972 presidential campaign, a hilarious (and entirely false) tale related in Thompson's "Fear and Loathing on the Campaign Trail."
 On the commentary track that accompanies the 2003 Criterion DVD release of Terry Gilliam's film adaptation of "Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas," Gilliam confirms that Adrenochrome is "a totally invented nonsense-drug that Hunter [made up]." Gilliam follows this with an eerily prescient statement that almost foreshadows the introduction of the drug into QAnon's ever-expanding mythology: "This scene had such an effect on so many people that afterwards I was hearing kids say, 'Oh, I've had Adrenochrome!' They were talking about where they get it. 'Oh, there's a guy who can get me Adrenochrome.' I just love how the Big Lie always works. It worked for Hitler, and it can work for people like us [i.e., artists and storytellers]."
 It appears to be working for QAnon as well, since "totally invented nonsense" has a way of becoming facts in the minds of the gullible, the semi-informed, the frustrated and the insane.
 So I did exactly as my friend suggested. I googled the word "Adrenochrome." Keep in mind that my friend insisted that Trump-supporting "white hats" had taken control of Google, so I would receive only   accurate  information from that search engine when inputting this particular word.
  The first result that popped up was Emily Writes' March 17, 2020, article in Spinoff entitled "    Down the Rabbit Hole With the COVID-19 Conspiracy Theorists ," which begins as follows:
 Today I fell down a rabbit hole of rabid Trump supporters who are convinced COVID-19 is both a hoax and also Trump's greatest moment as president. He has acted decisively apparently, while also knowing that COVID-19 is a Hollywood Liberal Elite cover-up ….   Adrenochrome  is a drug for the liberal elite of Hollywood made from actual human brain stem containing hormones from the adrenal gland. Hillary Clinton manufactures this drug by torturing children in a pizza shop …. Tom Hanks is addicted to Adrenochrome and he caught COVID-19 from the latest batch of tainted Adrenochrome that came through Celine Dion who is a high priestess from the Church of Satan. She is well-versed in poison as she's been lacing her children's clothing line with a chemical that makes our children "gender neutral." Tom Hanks signalled to the Hollywood Liberal Elite Cabal DeepState in his Golden Globes acceptance speech that there would be a shortage of Adrenochrome. Ellen has closed her studio audience because she's addicted as well.
 After reading this article in its entirety, I emailed my friend and pointed out that when I put his advice to the test and Googled "Adrenochrome," the first article that popped up sardonically tore the entire conspiracy theory to shreds. How did this jibe with the notion that Trump-supporting "white hats" had  total  control over Google?
 I received no direct answer to this question. I was told, instead, to search the name "Rachel Chandler." This would somehow answer   all  my questions. Bypassing those pesky "white hats" at Google, I decided to use a different search engine called Millionshort, and almost immediately found a March 22, 2019, Daily Dot article entitled "    QAnon Is Attacking a Random Woman in a Disturbing and Dangerous Way ," which detailed how QAnon posts had become strangely focused on a photographer and casting director named Rachel Chandler, who is part of the newspaper-owning Chandler family but has no visible political footprint and is not otherwise a public figure:
 Q made a seemingly endless stream of posts referencing Chandler, insinuating that she took a 2017 picture of Bill Clinton and registered child sex offender Jeffrey Epstein (whose light prison sentence handed down by current Trump cabinet official Alexander Acosta is currently under investigation) hanging out together in a pool.
 Beyond that, QAnon accused Chandler of "procuring" "girls" for Epstein, having her photography studio funded by elite Satanists who used it for rituals, flaunting Epstein's "sex and torture room victims" with her pictures, having a link to a still-unexplained (yet supposedly horrific) event at the Standard Hotel in West Hollywood ….
 To be clear, Chandler has not been accused of any wrongdoing by any law enforcement agency …. There's no evidence she took the pool picture that QAnon claims proves she's linked to Epstein, and it doesn't even feature him — though it does feature George Nader, the convicted pedophile who's providing evidence in the Mueller investigation regarding President Donald Trump's link to meetings with Russian officials.
 And yet Chandler's name has been dragged into the conspiracy so completely that the first page of a Google search shows nothing but QAnon-related content, while five of the top six videos that come up when searching her name are by QAnon acolytes.
  None of the many videos and speculations posted by QAnon and his numerous supporters provide any definitive links between Chandler and Jeffrey Epstein, yet somehow this red herring has received intense focus from thousands of amateur detectives, most of whom no doubt have a sincere desire to get to "the truth."
 One might argue, as does Emily Writes in her above-mentioned Spinoff article, that  when "people feel like they have no control over their lives — like in the midst of a global pandemic — the comforting certainty of conspiracy theories seems increasingly attractive."  One might also argue that racism, xenophobia and (perhaps most deadly of all)    neophobia  fuel the interest in such theories. But I think these explanations, while partially valid, ignore the heart-meat core of the real issue plaguing America at this moment.
 The real problem is that genuine conspiracies are unfolding before our eyes every day, but when the mainstream media avoids reporting on such conspiracies — for a whole variety of reasons, the main one being that it's simply easier to get paid rewriting corporate press releases and slapping one's byline on the end product than by actually putting one's reputation on the line tackling a plethora of inconvenient truths — it becomes necessary for average people to fill in the gaps on their own. These people may be ignorant, but they're not stupid. They know instinctively that they're being victimized by blatant lies every day. So, with no training whatsoever, they perform "research" on their own by scouring through a multiplicity of such "reliable" online sources as 4chan, 8chan, 8kun and Reddit.
   [W]hy is it so easy to find people who want to believe in conspiracies[?] My answer: because so many of the things that have traumatized so many people ARE conspiracies.
 The opioid epidemic was a conspiracy between rich families like the Sacklers and regulators who rotate in and out of industry. The 737 crisis was caused by Boeing's conspiracy to cut corners and aviation regulators' conspiracy to allow aerospace to regulate itself.
 Senators conspire to liquidate their positions ahead of coronavirus lockdown, well-heeled multinationals conspire to get 94.5% of the "small business" PPP fund, Big Tech conspires to fix wages with illegal collusion while fast food franchises do the same with noncompetes.
 In a world of constant real conspiracy scandals that destroy lives and the planet, conspiracy theories take on real explanatory power.
 Everything Doctorow says here is true; however, we rarely apply such clinical observations to people  we know     personally . Often, we use such explanations to dismiss the cockeyed beliefs of peculiar strangers encountered online. Before March of this year, I had known my friend to be a rational person with a fair amount of real-world experience and common sense, not someone who would be swayed easily by illogical rhetoric or over-the-top nonsense. On the other hand, I hadn't been in close contact with him since the last presidential election and the birth of QAnon, who emerged from internet obscurity in November of 2017.  This was, coincidentally, the same time period when Trump's approval ratings had reached a "near-record low."  (The full effect of QAnon on Trump's approval ratings since November of 2017 has not been studied in any substantial way.)
 After about four weeks had passed, I emailed my friend and asked him a few more questions about QAnon, trying to understand how anyone could believe so many unsubstantiated claims with so little skepticism. Surely, I thought, there must be something at least  halfway  reasonable to back up these outrageous statements. I needed to know the source so I could evaluate the claims myself. On May 3, I sent him the following email:
 I'm trying to wrap my head around this whole QAnon thing, so maybe you can help me. I have a few questions.
 Why would the United States military be "cleaning out" covert underground bases when they're the ones who   built  the bases in the first place? Who else but the U.S. military would be in charge of massive underground bases built on U.S. soil?
 Why would Trump be interested in implementing free energy? Isn't he trying to revive the coal industry? It would seem to me that free energy would undermine   all  of his business interests.
 According to most reliable sources, censorship on Google seems to be alive and well at the moment, which is nothing new. (In my experience, I've found that millionshort.com is a far better search engine tool.)
 I'm not certain that "white hats" exist in our current political situation. Perhaps "black hats," "slightly-less-black hats," and "gray hats" would be more accurate terms?
 Didn't the whole concept of "Adrenochrome" being harvested from live human beings originate with Hunter S. Thompson — as a satirical concept? I always thought that passage in "Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas" was intended as a joke. Is QAnon claiming that Hunter S. Thompson had "insider knowledge" when he wrote the book?
 The next day, my friend responded to my questions with another question: "You're a researcher. Why are you playing D.U.M.B.?" That's it. Nothing else.
 Since I had no idea why he had written the word "dumb" as an acronym, I replied, "I'm not playing 'D.U.M.B.' I honestly don't know the answers to those questions. I'm trying to understand the proposed scenario. If you have the answers, I'd love to hear them."
 Later that day, he wrote me back and explained that "D.U.M.B." stood for "Deep Underground Military Bases." The explanation rendered the first message even more confusing than before. (How could I possibly be playing "Deep Underground Military Bases"? I resisted the urge to ask him to elucidate further, as I suspected the response would just add to my befuddlement.) He sent me several links as well, despite the fact that a month earlier he had insisted that there    were no links  he could possibly send me. He never bothered to explain how or why this situation had changed during the past four weeks.
 Inevitably, the first link led me to QAnon's posts. Most of these posts were so cryptic, they didn't answer any of my questions.
 Addressing my question about Trump delivering free energy to the American people, my friend responded, "President Trump's uncle John was asked by the F.B.I. to investigate Nikola Tesla's papers." This terse sentence was then followed by a link to an Aug. 2, 2017, post at Heavy.com entitled " John Trump, Donald's Uncle: 5 Fast Facts You Need to Know." Right after the link, my friend wrote, "And you wonder where I come up with free energy for the nation in the future."
 Nothing    in the Heavy.com post suggested that Donald Trump was working on delivering free energy to the United States during his second term in office.
 Attempting to address one of my other questions, he wrote, "If Google's censorship is on, then why, when you search 'Rachel Chandler,' you get all of this information about her?"
 Of course this is a perfect example of "circular reasoning." Everything QAnon says about Rachel Chandler is true because, when you Google "Rachel Chandler," all of QAnon's posts pop up. Therefore, everything QAnon said about Rachel Chandler is true. (If A, then B. If B, then A. Or perhaps I should say, "If Q, then Q. If Q, then Q.")
 To answer my other questions, he sent me links to three different YouTube videos, one of which dealt with the untold story of the Deep Underground Military Bases.
 The first video (entitled "The Underground War, Happening Now") features a "Christian Patriot" named "Rick B2T" in conversation with an anonymous fellow calling himself "Gene." I subjected myself to 38 minutes and 16 seconds of unsubstantiated rumors about U.S. Christian soldiers battling demon-worshipping members of the Illuminati in deep underground bases.
 This directed me to an even more convoluted YouTube video entitled "    1 of 2 — Best of 'Underground War Details! Part 8' — Gene Decode — B2T Show ," in which I learned that Gene had decided to risk his life going public with these dangerous secrets because, one fateful day, God had contacted Gene and expressly told him, "This information    has  to come out!" At this point, Gene teamed up with an anonymous former "Canadian military officer" to reveal the disturbing truths about these "Deep Underground Military Bases" where (and this is a direct quote) "animal and human sacrifices nourish the bowels of creatures — in other words, we're talking demonic, terrible    things  that inhabited Earth long before Man arrived."
 After this startling revelation, Gene elaborated on the scenario further: "The New World Order is in the final stages — that is, they   were , until Trump came along — the final stage
......
title: 什么是QAnon？正在吞噬美国的阴谋论简介
title_en: What is QAnon? An introduction to the conspiracy theory that's eating America

note:
我个人的使命是让我们InfoSec社区的创建者做两件事：
这篇文章是关于其中的第二个，但让我们一次只看一个。
我在这里详细讨论了这一点，但TL；DR是所有平台都是短暂的，2)从长远来看，它们的需求总是放在您的需求之上-因为…。资本主义。
像Medium这样的内容平台代表了浮士德式的利益交换，现在是永恒的，以后也是痛苦的，作为一个自1999年以来目睹了这个周期数十次上演的人，我不会让你遭受这种痛苦。
这是一个很好的问题，希望每个人一开始都会问。如果你从一开始就在考虑钱，你的内容和受众规模可能会反映这一点。
但即使是热爱自己工作的人最终也会在精神上和经济上承受压力。成本开始增加：托管费、软件许可证、计算机和录制硬件等等。在某个时候，你开始问自己，你可以在不付钱的情况下维持多久。
所以这就是实际的一面-你会有成本，恢复它们会很好。但你应该在某个时候开始收费的真正原因是，这是你的艺术形式。这就是你要做的。你为此付出了很大的努力。你重视别人的工作，为什么他们不应该重视你的工作呢？
此外，还有一个考虑因素，即人们并不看重免费得到的东西，几乎与他们支付的东西一样-即使他们支付的不是很多。
这就是为什么你最终应该为你的创意水果收费的原因。但是，这种直接支持模式是什么？为什么它更好？
在我回答这个问题之前，让我先描述一下作为创造者的演变过程。正如我们在下面看到的，这项动议基本上是从实验-&>；非正式--严重--专业出发的。
我们可以改天再讨论所有这些方面，但人们通常会通过Twitch订阅或媒介等方式实现货币化。换句话说，通过第三方让你的观众与你的内容互动。
这很好，但是再说一遍-当你使用像这样的前端平台时，他们才是获得所有流量的人。他们是要中号的，不是给你的。他们是去Twitch，不是去你。因此，如果他们决定改变他们的商业模式，或者他们决定不喜欢你的内容，他们可能会把你的整个世界搞得一团糟。
解决此问题的方法是转向直接支持模式。直接支持意味着，考虑到技术的限制，你的观众会尽可能直接地给你钱。以下是一些平台示例及其相关的直接性级别。
对于Medium和Twitch，关系是平台和它们之间的关系。对于像Patreon这样的网站，他们仍然会去patreon.com查看内容。有了像Memberful这样的东西，你可以在你的网站上与你的观众互动，如果有人订阅了，钱就会直接进入你的手中。
在我看来，你应该考虑直接货币化的方式是从一个人到另一个人的增值基础设施。
我们都努力为世界提供价值，我们也理解为有价值的东西付钱。
有一种卖书的机制，但出版商仍然赚了太多的钱。卖音乐也是一样。传统的新闻业-出售你的作品-是一个死亡的过程，而通过博客获得报酬就像榨取鹅卵石一样。
在大多数情况下，问题在于抽象--有人在艺术家和观众之间穿梭。
这就是直接支持所做的事情。它使管道变得整洁。它是关于只有两个人重要的--你和一个欣赏你所做的事情的人。
因此，无论你是发布黑客视频，还是现场编程，还是展示如何制作最好的三明治，你都是在向宇宙喷射你的精髓，并问道：“有人能听到我吗？”
好吧，希望我已经说服你了。假设我有，下面是如何开始的。
如果你在YouTube上，你显然不能“移动”你的内容。
注册Memberful或Substack。如果你想要一个更长期、更灵活的盈利平台，我推荐Memberful。它不知道你是哪种类型的创造者：制造者、化妆、木工、时尚、黑客、制刀--它不在乎。子堆栈非常关注时事通讯的货币化。
创建一条从您的主项目(如时事通讯、播客或其他任何内容)到订阅站点的简单导航路径。
在您的所有社交媒体配置文件中链接到您的主要项目，例如，your site.com/podcast或your site.com/newsetter。
我真诚地希望在网上集市上看到你，把你的商品卖给任何觉得漂亮的人。
为艺术买单是人类交流的最高形式之一，我迫不及待地想让你参与进来。
我是十几个创作者的订阅者，通过Patreon、Memberful和其他直接的方式传播。
我推荐WordPress，Ghost和Hugo作为博客平台，按照你想要考虑平台还是内容的顺序。我更喜欢WordPress，因为它是最不碍事的，而且是以内容为中心的。是的，我是推荐WordPress的保安。现在它实际上是相当安全的--只要你小心使用插件。
我想感谢萨姆·哈里斯为这件事做了这么多方面的工作。正是他唤醒了我直达的选择，随着这个空间的发展，我继续从他的实现中学习。
你需要一些中等的技术技能来完成其中的几个步骤，从博客平台的东西到连接支付平台。你要么自己解决这个问题，要么你需要雇个人。
note_en:
I’m on a personal mission to get the creators in our InfoSec community to do two things:
  This piece is about the second of those, but let’s take them one at a time.
  I covered this extensively  here, but the TL;DR is that all platforms are 1) ephemeral, and 2) will always place their needs over yours over the longterm—because…Capitalism.
  Content platforms like Medium represent the timeless Faustian exchange of benefits now and suffering later, and as someone who’s seen this cycle play out dozens of times since 1999, I would spare you that pain.
  This is a great question that hopefully everyone asks in the beginning. If you’re thinking about money from the start, your content and audience size might reflect that.
 But even people who love what they do eventually end up stressing themselves mentally and financially. The costs start adding up: Hosting fees, software licenses, computer and recording hardware, etc. At some point you start asking yourself how long you can maintain this without getting paid for it.
  So that’s the practical side—you’re going to have costs, and it’d be nice to recuperate them. But the real reason you should start charging at some point is that it’s your art form. It’s what you do. And you put  a lot of effort into it. You value other peoples’ work, so why shouldn’t they value yours?
 Plus, there is the consideration that people don’t value what comes freely nearly as much as something they pay for—even if they’re not paying much.
  So that’s why you should eventually charge for your creative fruit. But what’s this Direct Support model, and why is it better?
 Well, before I answer that, let me describe the evolution of being a creator. The motion basically goes from Experimental –> Casual –> Serious –> Professional, as we see below.
  We can talk about all these dimensions another time, but people usually get into monetization through something like Twitch Subscriptions or Medium. In other words, through a third party where your audience is interacting with your content.
 That’s fine, but again—when you use a front-end platform like this, they’re the ones getting all the traffic. They’re going to Medium, not to you. They’re going to Twitch, not to you. So if they decide to change their business model, or they decide they don’t like your content, they can mess up your entire world.
  The way to get around this is to move to a Direct Support model. Direct Support means your audience is giving you the money as directly as possible, given the limitations of technology. Here are some examples of platforms along with their associated directness levels.
         With Medium and Twitch, the relationship is between the platform and them. With something like Patreon, they still go to patreon.com to see the content. And with something like Memberful you can interact with your audience on  yoursite.com, and if someone subscribes the money comes to you directly.
  The way you should think about direct monetization—in my opinion—is Appreciation Infrastructure from one human to another.
 We all strive to provide value to the world, and we also understand paying for things that have value.
  There’s a mechanism for selling books, but publishers still make way too much of the money. Same with selling music. Traditional journalism—selling your writing—is the process of dying, and getting paid for blogging is like milking a pebble.
 In most of these cases, the problem is abstraction—someone getting between the artist and the audience.
 That’s what Direct Support does. It unclutters that pipeline. It makes it about the only two people who matter—you and someone who appreciates what you do.
 So whether you’re putting out hacking videos, or live programming, or showing how to make the best sandwich—you are blasting your essence into the universe and asking, “Can anyone hear me?”
   Ok, well hopefully I’ve convinced you. Assuming I have, here’s how to get started.
 If you’re on YouTube you obviously can’t “move” your content.
 Sign up with Memberful or Substack. I recommend Memberful if you want a longer-term, more flexible platform for monetization. It’s agnostic to what type of creator you are: maker, makeup, woodworking, fashion, hacking, knife-making—it doesn’t care. Substack is very focused on newsletter monetization.
  Create an easy navigation path from your main project—like your newsletter or your podcast or whatever—to your subscription site.
 Link to your main project in all your social media profiles, e.g., yoursite.com/podcast, or yoursite.com/newsletter.
 I honestly hope to see you out there in the internet bazaar, selling your wares to anyone who finds them beautiful.
 Paying for art is among the highest forms of human exchange, and I can’t wait for you to participate.
  I am a subscriber to over a dozen creators in this way, spread across Patreon, Memberful, and other direct methods.
   I recommend WordPress, Ghost, and Hugo for blogging platforms, in order of how much you want to think about the platform vs. the content. I prefer WordPress because it’s the most out of your way and content-focused. And yes, I’m a security person recommending WordPress. It’s actually decently secure these days—as long as you’re careful with plugins.
 I want to give my thanks to Sam Harris for so many aspects of this. It was he who woke me to the option of going direct, and I continue to learn from his implementations as this space evolves.
 You will need some moderate technical skills to do a few of these steps, from the blogging platform stuff to connecting the payment platforms. You can either hack that out yourself or you’ll need to hire someone.
title: 创建者应该转移到自己的域名并建立直接货币化
title_en: Creators Should Move to Their Own Domains and Set Up Direct Monetization

几十年来，物理学家已经注意到某些金属材料在电阻(对电流的迟滞量)上表现出奇怪的行为。
通常情况下，金属越热，电子从金属的振动原子上反弹的越多，使得电流更难通过。通常情况下，当金属冷却时，振动减弱，电子移动更容易，电阻下降。
但有时，当一些金属材料被冷却得更冷时，电阻又开始上升。那是个谜。
这种现象现在被称为近藤效应，以日本物理学家近藤俊(Jun Kondo)的名字命名，他成功地解释了这一现象。近藤效应已被证明是理解电子在固体中行为所需的中心成分。
但是，作为一名理论家而不是实验家，近藤博士并不是第一个证明他的假设是正确的。
相反，现年87岁的萨拉奇克博士在纽约城市学院(City College Of New York)担任了半个多世纪的物理学教授后，现已退休。
这项实验只是萨拉奇克博士今年获得美国物理学会(American Physical Society)最高荣誉--杰出研究成就奖章(Medal For Extrational Achievement In Research)的成就之一。
“所以我就来了，”萨拉奇克博士1月份在华盛顿举行的一个仪式上说。“我简直不敢相信，因为我差点就没进过赛场。”
她的职业生涯始于1957年苏联发射人造卫星Sputnik和1961年第一位宇航员尤里·加加林(Yuri Gagarin)之后，美国正在竞相追赶科学和太空。大学正在建立和扩大他们的物理系。行业就业机会很多。
但是，即使在这个科学的黄金时代，像萨拉奇克博士这样的女性也不被鼓励参与其中。
在完成近藤效应研究的两年前，萨拉奇克博士屈从于当时的期望，在纽约哥伦比亚大学(Columbia University)完成博士学位一年后，搁置了她的物理研究。她打算呆在家里照顾她刚出生的女儿凯伦。
“我在家里呆了大约一个月，我意识到我再也活不下去了，”她说。
她的丈夫菲利普(Philip)是哥伦比亚大学电气工程的助理教授，他敦促她重返研究领域。她记得他说过，“我宁愿雇人来照顾凯伦，也不愿请精神病医生。”
但是，当萨拉奇克博士在纽约的一个物理会议上参加招聘会时，与她的哥伦比亚同学不同的是，她没有收到任何求职面试请求。“我一个都没有，”她说。“我完全得了零分。我又一次非常不开心。所以非常非常不开心。“。
“我请他帮帮我，”萨拉奇克博士说。“他和我争论了很长时间，而且很激烈。他说，‘你并不是真的想做你认为你想做的事情。你不会想做研究的。也许你应该做一份兼职教书的工作。我说，‘不，我想做研究。’“。
她说，在来回辩论的最后，库什博士让步了：“最后他说，‘看，米里亚姆，我们训练了你。我不知道你为什么想做你想做的事。但如果你想做，你有权去尝试。‘“
在一次采访中，Sarachik博士在谈到Kusch博士时说，“他有这种偏见。我们都有，但他愿意在上面操作。“。
第二天，她接到美国电话电报公司(AT&amp；T)在新泽西州的研究机构贝尔实验室(Bell Labs)打来的电话。贝尔实验室当时是突破性基础研究的圣地。萨拉奇克博士说，她被录用的职位相当于两年的博士后职位。
贝尔实验室的其他物理学家正在研究含有铌、钼和铼等金属以及少量铁的合金。少量的铁有时在材料中起到磁铁的作用。
萨拉奇克博士研究了这些磁性合金的一个方面，测量了电阻如何随温度变化，从接近绝对零度的零下459度(可能的最低温度)到大约零下350华氏度。
测量电阻很简单--学生们可能会在高中物理实验室里做这件事。对于专业实验室来说，即使是超低温也不是一个技术挑战。
当铁的磁性存在时，她观察到电阻随温度下降而增加的莫名其妙的行为。当磁场不存在时，随着温度的降低，电阻继续下降。
这一结果引起了近藤博士的注意，他提出的计算表明，随着合金冷却，电子从铁原子的磁铁上散射的越来越多，从而增加了电阻。
萨拉奇克博士说，当近藤博士寄给她他的论文的早期版本时，她立刻知道她的数据符合他的计算。因此，她首次在实验上证实了近藤效应。
但她的贡献在很大程度上被其他人忽视了，包括她在贝尔实验室的同事。
“多年来我一直没有得到认可，”她说，很快她就开始找新工作了。
萨拉奇克博士说，在实验室的员工排名中，她被排在倒数三分之一，当她的两年任期结束时，没有人邀请她留下来。几乎在同一时间，她的丈夫没有获得哥伦比亚大学的终身教职，也需要一份新的工作，所以他们两人考虑搬离纽约。
如今，已婚学者经常谈论两体问题-在寻找愿意同时聘用这两个人的大学或公司时，在职业生涯之间权衡。这可能是一个错综复杂的平衡行动，但它是司空见惯的。在20世纪60年代，这是罕见的。只有丈夫才重要。事实上，给妻子提供一份工作通常被认为是任人唯亲。
菲利普·萨拉奇克(Philip Sarachik)收到了加入密歇根大学(University Of Michigan)和马里兰大学(University Of Marland)等一流大学教师的邀请。她只收到了马里兰大学提供的一个临时博士后职位。
当时位于纽约市北部的飞利浦研究实验室为她提供了一份工作。但是薪水会比一个人的收入低几千美元。她表示反对；该公司告诉她，这是该行业对女性的普遍做法。她拒绝了这一提议，尽管当时她别无选择。
“我不是一个半途而废的人，”萨拉奇克博士说。“我不能不这么做。”
在整个流浪的童年里，她经常不能融入其中。她出生在比利时安特卫普，父母是东正教犹太教徒，当时阿道夫·希特勒(Adolf Hitler)正在邻国德国掌权。通过伪造文件、行贿和逃离集中营，她和她的父母以及两个兄弟逃走了，先是去了古巴，然后去了纽约市。她是首批进入布朗克斯科学高中的女孩之一，然后她进入了巴纳德学院，在哥伦比亚大学上了物理课。
当她决定学习物理时，科学正慢慢地向女性开放。“如果我早20年尝试，我认为这是不可能的，”她说。
她的丈夫也鼓励她，他是在她的一次本科物理课上认识的。“他非常支持我，”她说。“他给了我做我真正想做的事情的空间。”
1964年，当萨拉奇克博士找不到工作时，菲利普说，在她也在同一地区找到工作之前，很容易决定把他提供的工作转给她。“做出这样的选择有什么困难？”他说。“我在很多地方都有工作机会，所以我有选择，而Myriam没有，所以选择一个我们都有工作的地方并不是很困难。”
最后，1964年，当菲利普加入纽约大学时，纽约城市学院为她提供了一个助理教授的职位。在三年的时间里，她被提升为终身教职的副教授。她的事业蒸蒸日上。
萨拉奇克博士在1月份的演讲中略微提到了这一损失。她说：“我们经历了一场灾难性的家庭灾难，这让我几乎失去了10到15年的工作。”“你们中的一些人知道这一点。”
1970年秋季学期开始后不久，保姆开着这对夫妇的车去接约会结束的莉亚，再也没有回来。当时9岁的大女儿凯伦(Karen)独自一人在家。12天后，当局发现这名保姆死于过量的安眠药，死于汽车后部。
一个月后，利亚的身体在佛蒙特州一栋避暑别墅后面的垃圾桶里被发现。
萨拉奇克医生在她公寓的墙上挂满了针线活。她帮助她的研究生完成了他们的学位。她教了几节课。
在20世纪80年代，当她试图重新开始她的努力时，她在城市学院物理系的一个实验室已经变成了事实上的垃圾储藏室。她发出了一份备忘录，要求人们带走自己的财物。没有人这么做。她发出了另一份备忘录，称任何在本周末之前不清除的东西都将被扔掉。
佛罗里达州塔拉哈西国家高磁场实验室的首席科学家劳拉·H·格林(Laura H.Greene)大约在这个时候第一次见到了萨拉奇克博士。在萨拉奇克博士在贝尔实验室工作20年后，格林博士刚刚加入了贝尔实验室。
格林博士说：“我不知道她刚刚重返物理学。”“我知道她对自己有一种悲伤。”
格林博士正在从一个不同的物理领域转行，他需要了解萨拉奇克博士的许多工作。“她有无限的耐心，”格林博士说。“真的很会解释事情。”
她探索了一些二维材料(通常是不导电的绝缘体)是如何变成金属导体的，这是理论家所说的不可能的。
“这远远超前了，”格林博士说。“所以她总是在挑战极限。”
对于她所展示的，理论家们确实还没有提出令人信服的解释。
萨拉奇克博士还领导了探索像磁铁一样的分子量子行为的实验。这项工作表明，这些分子的北极和南极，每个都由几百个原子组成，可以在寒冷的温度下自发翻转，而这种翻转是经典物理学所禁止的。
其他物理学家也曾试图证明这一点。但在当时，这些材料只能制成粉末。这些晶体斑点的磁场指向随机的方向，证据并不确凿。
萨拉奇克博士的一名学生乔纳森·弗里德曼(Jonathan Friedman)提供了一种解决方案，将粉末混合到液体胶中，并将混合物置于强磁场中。晶体与磁场排成一排，当胶水干了之后，仍然指向那个方向。
雷曼学院(Lehman College)和纽约城市大学研究生中心(City University of New York‘s Graduate Center)的物理学教授尤金·丘德诺夫斯基(Eugene Chudnovsky)说，“它以大型研讨会开始了整个领域，最终有数千名理论家和实验者在这一领域工作。”
“她之所以是一名优秀的物理学家，是因为她非常聪明，”丘德诺夫斯基博士说，他写过一封信，提名萨拉奇克博士获得美国物理学会奖章(American Physical Society Medal)。
“在做物理方面，女性并不比男性好，也不比男性差，”她说。“然而，至少在我这个年纪，他们更顽强。那就是坚韧。这是不被推出去的意志。“
note_en:
For decades, physicists had noticed certain metallic materials exhibited odd behavior in electrical resistance — the amount of sluggishness to the flow of electricity.
 Usually, the warmer a metal is, the more that electrons bounce off the metal’s vibrating atoms, making it more difficult for a current to pass through. Typically, as a metal cools, the vibrations diminish, the electrons move more readily, and the resistance drops.
 But sometimes as some metallic materials are chilled even colder, the electrical resistance starts rising again. It was a mystery.
 The phenomenon is now known as the Kondo effect, after Jun Kondo, a Japanese physicist who successfully explained what was going on. The Kondo effect has turned out to be a central component needed to understand the behavior of electrons in solids.
 But Dr. Kondo, as a theorist and not an experimentalist, was not the first to show that his supposition was correct.
 That instead was Dr. Sarachik, 87, now retired after a career spanning more than a half-century as a professor of physics at the City College of New York.
 The experiment was just one of the accomplishments for which Dr. Sarachik received this year’s Medal for Exceptional Achievement in Research, a top honor of the American Physical Society.
 “And so here I am,” Dr. Sarachik said during a ceremony held in Washington in January. “I can’t even believe it, because I almost didn’t get into the field at all.”
 Her career commenced as the United States was racing to catch up in science and space after the Soviets launched the Sputnik satellite in 1957 and the first astronaut, Yuri Gagarin, in 1961. Colleges were establishing and expanding their physics departments. Industry jobs were bountiful.
 But even during this golden era of science, women like Dr. Sarachik were discouraged from participating.
 Two years before her Kondo effect work, Dr. Sarachik, giving in to the expectations of the day,  set aside her physics research a year after finishing her Ph.D. at Columbia University in New York. She was going to stay home and take care of Karen, her newborn daughter.
 “I was home for about a month, and I realized I was never going to survive this,” she said.
 Her husband, Philip, an assistant professor of electrical engineering at Columbia, urged her to return to research. She recalled him saying, “I would rather pay someone to take care of Karen than a psychiatrist.”
 But when she attended a job fair at a physics conference in New York, Dr. Sarachik, unlike her Columbia classmates, received no requests for job interviews. “I got none,” she said. “I got absolutely zero. I got, again, very unhappy. So very, very unhappy.”
  “I asked him to please help me,” Dr. Sarachik said. “He argued with me long and hard. He said, ‘You don’t really want to do what you think you want to do. You don’t want to do research. Maybe you should take a part-time teaching job.’ And I said, ‘No, I want to do research.’”
 She said that at the end of the back-and-forth, Dr. Kusch gave in: “Finally he said, ‘Look, Myriam, we trained you. I don’t know why you want to do what you want to do. But if you want to do it, you have the right to try.’”
 In an interview, Dr. Sarachik said of Dr. Kusch, “He had this bias. We all have it, but he was willing to operate above it.”
 The next day, she received a phone call from Bell Labs, the research arm of AT&T in New Jersey, which was then a mecca for groundbreaking basic research. Dr. Sarachik said she was hired to what amounted to a two-year postdoctoral position.
  Other physicists at Bell Labs were exploring alloys containing metals like niobium, molybdenum and rhenium as well as a smidgen of iron. The small amounts of iron sometimes acted as magnets within the materials.
 Dr. Sarachik investigated an aspect of these magnetic alloys, measuring how the electrical resistance changed with temperature, from close to absolute zero, minus 459 degrees, the coldest possible temperature, to about minus 350 degrees Fahrenheit.
 Measuring electrical resistance is straightforward — something that students might do in a high school physics laboratory. Even the ultralow temperatures were not a technical challenge for a professional laboratory.
  When the magnetism of the iron was present, she observed the unexplained behavior of resistance increasing with falling temperatures. When the magnetism was not present, the resistance continued to drop with the dropping temperatures.
 The results caught the eye of Dr. Kondo, who had come up with calculations that suggested that as the alloy cooled, the electrons scattered more and more off the magnets of iron atoms, increasing the electrical resistance.
 Dr. Sarachik said that when Dr. Kondo sent her an early version of his paper, she immediately knew that her data fit with his calculations. She thus provided the first experimental confirmation of the Kondo effect.
 But her contribution was largely overlooked by others, including by her colleagues at Bell Labs.
 “I got no recognition for it for years,” she said, and soon she was looking for a new job.
 Dr. Sarachik said in the laboratory’s rankings of employees, she was placed in the bottom third and when her two-year appointment ended, there was no offer for her to stay. At about the same time, her husband did not receive tenure at Columbia and also needed a new job, so the two of them considered moving away from New York.
 These days, married academics often talk of the two-body problem — the juggling of careers in the search of a university or company willing to hire both. It can be a convoluted balancing act, but it is commonplace. In the 1960s, that was rare. Only the husband mattered. Indeed, offering a job to the wife as well was often regarded as nepotism.
 Philip Sarachik received offers to join the faculty at top-tier universities like the University of Michigan and the University of Maryland. She only received one offer of a temporary postdoctoral position, from the University of Maryland.
 Philips Research Laboratories, then located just north of New York City, offered her a job. But the salary would have been thousands of dollars below what a man would earn. She objected; the company told her that was the prevailing practice toward women in the industry. She rejected the offer, even though she had no other options at the time.
 “I’m not a quitter,” Dr. Sarachik said. “I couldn’t not do it.”
  Throughout a peripatetic childhood, she often did not fit in. She was born in Antwerp, Belgium, to Orthodox Jewish parents, just as Adolf Hitler was rising to power in neighboring Germany. Through false papers, bribes and an escape from a concentration camp, she, her parents and two brothers fled, first to Cuba and then to New York City. She was among the first girls to attend the Bronx High School of Science, and then she went to Barnard College, taking physics classes at Columbia.
 The sciences were opening up to women, slowly, when she decided to study physics. “If I had tried 20 years earlier, I don’t think it would have been possible,” she said.
 Her husband, whom she met in one of her undergraduate physics classes, encouraged her, too. “He was enormously supportive of me,” she said. “He gave me the room to do what I really wanted to do.”
 When Dr. Sarachik was having trouble finding a job in 1964, Philip said it was an easy decision to pass on the offers he had until she also found a position in the same locale. “What’s the difficulty in making that kind of choice?” he said. “I had offers in a number of places so I had choices when Myriam didn’t, so it wasn’t very difficult to choose a place where we both had jobs.”
 Finally, the City College of New York offered her a position as an assistant professor in 1964 while Philip joined New York University. In three years, she was promoted to associate professor with tenure. Her career thrived.
  Dr. Sarachik mentioned the loss glancingly during her talk in January. “We had a disastrous family disaster, which took me out of commission pretty much for 10, 15 years,” she said. “And some of you know about that.”
 Soon after the start of the fall semester in 1970, the nanny drove the couple’s car to pick up Leah from a play date and never returned. The older daughter, Karen, then 9, was at home alone. Twelve days later, authorities found the nanny dead in the rear of the car from an overdose of sleeping pills.
 A month later, Leah’s body was found in a trash can behind a summer house in Vermont.
 Dr. Sarachik filled the walls of her apartment with needlework. She helped her graduate students finish their degrees. She taught some classes.
  In the 1980s, as she sought to restart her efforts, one of her laboratories at the City College physics department had become a de facto storage room of junk. She sent out a memo asking people to take their belongings. No one did. She sent out another memo saying that anything not removed by the end of the week would be thrown out.
  Laura H. Greene, chief scientist at the National High Magnetic Field Laboratory in Tallahassee, Fla., first met Dr. Sarachik around this time. Dr. Greene had just joined Bell Labs, two decades after Dr. Sarachik worked there.
 “I didn’t know she was just getting back into physics,” Dr. Greene said. “I knew she had a sadness about her.”
 Dr. Greene was switching from a different area of physics and needed to learn about a lot of Dr. Sarachik’s work. “She had infinite patience,” Dr. Greene said. “Really good at explaining things.”
 She explored how some two-dimensional materials, generally insulators that do not conduct electricity, could turn into metallic conductors, something theorists said was impossible.
 “That’s way ahead of the time,” Dr. Greene said. “So she was always pushing the envelope.”
  Theorists indeed still have not come up with a convincing explanation for what she showed.
 Dr. Sarachik also led experiments that explored the quantum behavior of molecules that act like magnets. The work demonstrated that the north and south poles of these molecules, each consisting of a couple hundred atoms, could spontaneously flip at cold temperatures where such flips are forbidden by classical physics.
 Other physicists had tried to show this as well. But at that time, the materials could only be made as powders. The magnetic fields of these crystal specks pointed in random directions, and the evidence was inconclusive.
 One of Dr. Sarachik students, Jonathan Friedman, provided a solution by mixing the powder in a liquid glue and placing the mixture in a strong magnetic field. The crystals lined up with the magnetic field, and as the glue dried, remained pointing in that direction.
  “It started the whole field with big symposiums, ending up with thousands of theorists and experimentalists working in this area,” said Eugene Chudnovsky, a professor of physics at Lehman College and the City University of New York’s Graduate Center.
 “The reason she is a good physicist is because she’s very clever,” said Dr. Chudnovsky, who wrote a letter nominating Dr. Sarachik for the American Physical Society medal.
  “Women are no better and no worse at doing physics than men are,” she said. “They are, however, at least if they’re my age, more persistent. It’s tenacity. It’s the will not to be pushed out.”
title: Myriam Sarachik从未放弃物理
title_en: Myriam Sarachik Never Gave Up on Physics

note:
C正确地赞扬了口罩的优点，赞扬了通风的必要性，并赞扬了户外活动的有益性质。但另一种行为策略没有得到足够的关注，部分原因是它通过缺席来展示自己。这种策略就是沉默。
是的，在这场大流行中，终于到了谈论在这场大流行中不说话的重要性的时候了。
科罗拉多大学博尔德分校(University Of Colorado At Boulder)研究疾病传播的教授何塞·L·希门尼斯(Jose L.Jimenez)告诉我，“如果我们在公共场所少说话或说话声音小一些，每条病毒传播途径都会下降。”“这只是一个非常清楚的事实。这是没有争议的。“。
沉默作为一种抗病毒策略是金色的，因为这种疾病是如何传播的。冠状病毒似乎主要通过当我们打喷嚏、咳嗽、说话或唱歌时从我们脸上喷发的病毒颗粒传播。其中一些颗粒足够重，可以溅到附近的表面或迅速落到地面。这些被称为大液滴。它们只是与从我们嘴里喷出并以漩涡粒子云的形式在空气中徘徊的较小的球体相比才算大。这些被称为气溶胶。
病毒颗粒搭乘这些飞沫和气溶胶离开我们的身体。它们在空气中传播--几秒钟的水滴；几分钟的气溶胶--并且可以停留在其他人的鼻子、喉咙和肺部。因此，通过我们呼气的自由传递，病毒传播到一个新的宿主。
新冠肺炎公共卫生指导的目标是关闭这个病毒传播系统：良好的通风驱散气溶胶云；保持距离降低其他人对这些颗粒物的易感性；戴上口罩阻止它们进入我们的嘴巴和鼻子。
但是，如果我们试图在这些携带病毒的颗粒离开我们的身体之前就限制它们的产生呢？这就是音量控制的用武之地。
少说话、多说话或根本不说话都会限制大液滴和气溶胶的制造。当你呼吸或窃窃私语时，你的呼吸系统不会释放大水滴。希门尼斯告诉我，与大喊大叫相比，安静的谈话减少了5倍的气溶胶；完全沉默减少了大约50倍的气溶胶。这意味着安静地交谈，而不是大喊大叫，可以将病毒传播的风险降低到与正确戴口罩相当的程度。
希门尼斯说：“事实是，如果每个人都停止说话一两个月，大流行很可能就会消失。”在强调距离的重要性的同时，他阐述说，如果没有任何密切接触的交流，这种病毒将很难在人与人之间继续传播。(测试这篇论文最极端的版本是不可取的，方法是形成一个无声的马什坑，或者当你走在街上时向四面八方吐口水。)。
希门尼斯并不是唯一一个在封闭的岛屿上敲打鼓的科学家。其他人同意，一点点的言论管制可能会对减少病毒传播大有帮助。马里兰大学公共卫生学院(University Of Marland School Of Public Health)教授唐纳德·K·米尔顿(Donald K.Milton)研究过医用口罩如何减少病毒传播，他在一封电子邮件中告诉我，“沉默和轻声说话是合理的干预手段”，以减少新冠肺炎的传播。
这场大流行的奇特国际成功故事是日本。虽然世界上许多国家都强调测试和跟踪的重要性，但日本最初没有大规模测试，也没有复杂的合同跟踪手段。然而，经人口调整后，中国目前新冠肺炎的死亡人数比美国大约少98%。很多功劳都归功于它明确的公共指导，避免了三个C：封闭的空间，拥挤的地方，近距离接触的环境，包括“近距离对话”。
这第三个C值得更多的阐述。即使在交通客运量恢复正常后，日本仍报告称，东京以拥挤著称的地铁没有爆发疫情。这座城市的火车通常通风良好，日本人几十年来一直在练习戴口罩。但这里还有其他一些因素在起作用：日本通勤者有一条非正式的规则，即使有的话，也要避免在火车上大声交谈。
因此，虽然纽约市每月花费1500万美元在地铁上喷洒抗菌剂，但日本人用一种更便宜的策略来保证火车的安全：遮盖和关闭。
如果美国人将日本的地铁规则引入我们的公共生活，我们或许能够加速回归一种更为温和的常态。在过去的几周里，我多次听到人们在杂货店和药店用手机大声交谈，有时会把口罩从嘴里推到一边，以提高措辞的清晰度。这样的行为表明完全没有理解疾病是如何传播的。
这里有一个解决方案：美国的图书馆规则。每次你走进一所学校、一家诊所、一家药店、一家理发店、一间办公室、一架飞机、一列火车或一座政府大楼，你都应该看到一块写着“或或”的牌子。希门尼斯告诉我：“就科学而言，我相信像这条图书馆规则这样的东西会减少病毒传播的所有方式。”
标志和指导只能起到这么大的作用。一些美国人(主要是保守派男性)对口罩的强烈反对表明，有一部分人可能会反抗图书馆的规定，比如，拍摄自己故意对着咖啡师的脸大喊大叫的视频。
希门尼斯说：“我们从戴面具的经历中学到的是，许多美国人不喜欢别人告诉他们，如果他们不明白为什么有必要的话，他们应该怎么做。”“人们需要明白，这种病毒存在于空气中，当他们大声叫喊或大声说话时，他们呼出的病毒是空气中病毒的10倍。”
大多数美国人不习惯于考虑这场大流行的任何听觉层面。例如，网上的耻辱者经常发布表面上拥挤的海滩的照片。这些快照没有捕捉到几个重要的因素，比如人们站在一起的时间有多长。最重要的是，它们没有告诉我们任何关于声音的信息。那些日光浴者是在一把大伞下互相叫嚷着海滩礼仪，还是静静地沉浸在一本书里？在一场通常通过空气传播的瘟疫中，我们互动的声音场景与我们行为的照片图像一样重要。
对呼气科学和气溶胶的高度关注可能会帮助人们了解哪些环境出人意料地安全，哪些环境出人意料地危险。在许多城市，餐馆比电影院恢复得更快。但是，与室内餐厅相比，座位分散、戴着通用口罩、完全安静的电影院可能风险较小，在室内餐厅，用餐者不戴口罩交谈和咀嚼。“我不能在追踪接触者的文献中说出电影院爆发的名字，”希门尼斯说，同时也强调电影院不能被认为是无风险的，因为电影院是通风不良的室内空间，人们连续几个小时都在那里度过。“我可以告诉你，剧院看起来远没有吵闹的餐厅或酒吧那么危险，在那里人们必须大声说话才能被听到。”
相比之下，如果每个人都在虔诚的沉默中锻炼，健身房可能看起来很安全。但意大利卡西诺大学(University Of Cassino)和南拉齐奥大学(Southern Lazio)的研究人员对呼气流动进行的一项研究估计，大多数人在高强度锻炼时呼吸时排出的病毒颗粒比站着说话时更多。在高强度锻炼后正常交谈可以排出与站着休息时叫喊一样多的病毒颗粒。这些估计是粗略的，因人而异。但显而易见的是，在拥挤的房间里在固定自行车上气喘吁吁是有风险的，即使你从来没有说过一句话。
呼气减缓不能在真空中考虑。这是消灭病毒的整体方法的一部分，同时谨慎地恢复那些给我们的社区和城市带来欢乐、意义和社区(是的，还有消费者收入)的活动。
保护人们安全的最好方法是让新冠肺炎了解病毒传播的全部过程，并在这个过程中的每一步都反向工程一个障碍。想象一下，库尔特·冯内古特(Kurt Vonnegut)式的病毒传播子弹从新感染的宿主中倒流出来。被病毒浸泡的气溶胶会从患者的肺部或鼻腔中排出。它神奇地穿过他们的鼻子，进入空气中，在那里逗留了几分钟，然后被一个没有戴口罩的人吸入，他在人群中向后喊着胡言乱语。
我们可以在这个故事的每个阶段进行干预，用口罩来阻止病毒，用社交距离来分隔人群，用通风来驱散气溶胶。但我们为什么要忽视创始事件，也就是病毒子弹的铸造呢？节省你的声音；拯救一条生命。
我们想听听您对这篇文章的看法。向编辑提交一封信，或写信给letters@theatlantic.com。
是“大西洋月刊”的特约撰稿人，在那里他撰写关于经济、技术和媒体的文章。他是热门人物的作者，也是播客“疯狂/天才”(Crazy/Genius)的主持人。
note_en:
C  have rightly extolled  the virtues of masks, hailed  the necessity of ventilation, and praised  the salutary nature of outdoor activities. But another behavioral tactic hasn’t received enough attention, in part because it makes itself known by its absence. That tactic is silence.
  Yes, it is finally time to talk, in this pandemic, about the importance of not talking in this pandemic.
  “Every route of viral transmission would go down if we talked less, or talked less loudly, in public spaces,” Jose L. Jimenez, a professor at the University of Colorado at Boulder, who studies disease transmission, told me. “This is just a very clear fact. It’s not controversial.”
 Silence is golden as an antiviral strategy because of how this disease spreads. The coronavirus seems to move primarily through viral particles that erupt from our faces when we sneeze, cough, talk, or sing. Some of these particles are heavy enough to splash on a nearby surface or fall quickly to the ground. Those are called  large droplets. They are large only in comparison with the smaller globs that spray from our mouths and linger in the air in a swirling particle cloud. These are called  aerosols.
    Viral particles hitch a ride out of our bodies on these droplets and aerosols. They travel through the air—droplets, for a few seconds; aerosols, for several minutes—and can lodge in the noses, throats, and lungs of other people. Thus conveyed by the free transit of our exhalations, the virus travels to a new host.
  The goal of COVID-19 public-health guidance is to shut down this viral transit system: Good ventilation disperses aerosol clouds; distancing reduces other people’s vulnerability to these particles; masks block them from our mouths and noses.
 But what if we tried to limit the production of these virus-laden particles before they even left our bodies? That’s where volume control comes in.
 Talking less, more quietly, or not at all limits the manufacture of both large droplets and aerosols. When you breathe or whisper, your respiratory system doesn’t emit large droplets. Jimenez told me that, compared with yelling, quiet talking reduces aerosols by a factor of five; being completely silent reduces them by a factor of about 50. That means talking quietly, rather than yelling, reduces the risk of viral transmission by a degree  comparable to properly wearing a mask.
  “The truth is that if everybody stopped talking for a month or two, the pandemic would probably die off,” Jimenez said. While still emphasizing the importance of distancing, he elaborated that without any close-contact communication, it would be difficult for this virus to continue moving between people. (It’s unadvisable to test the most extreme version of this thesis by forming a silent mosh pit, or spitting in every direction when you walk down the street.)
 Jimenez is not the only scientist banging the drums on Shut-Up Island. Others agree that a little bit of speech policing could go a long way to reducing viral transmission. Donald K. Milton, a professor at the University of Maryland School of Public Health, who  has studied how surgical face masks can reduce viral spread, told me in an email that “silence and quiet speaking are reasonable means of intervening” to reduce COVID-19 transmission.
 O  curious international success stories of the pandemic is Japan. While much of the world emphasized  the importance of testing and tracing, Japan  initially had no mass testing and no sophisticated means of contract tracing. Yet, adjusted for population, the country currently has  approximately 98 percent fewer COVID-19 deaths than the United States. Much credit has gone to its crystal-clear public guidance to avoid the  three C’s: closed spaces, crowded places, and close-contact settings, including “close-range conversations.”
   That third C deserves more amplification. Even after its transit ridership  bounced back to normal, Japan reported no outbreaks on Tokyo’s famously crowded subways. The city’s trains are typically well ventilated, and Japanese people have had decades of practice wearing masks. But something else is at work here: Japanese commuters have  an informal rule to avoid talking loudly on trains, if at all.
    So while New York City  spends $15 million a month blasting its subways with antimicrobial sprays, the Japanese keep their trains safe with a cheaper tactic: masking up and shutting up.
 If Americans imported Japan’s subway rules to our public life, we might be able to accelerate our return to a more muted form of normalcy. Several times in the past few weeks, I’ve heard people talking loudly on their cellphones in grocery stores and pharmacies, sometimes with their masks pushed aside from their mouths to improve the clarity of their diction. Such behavior displays a total failure to understand how the disease spreads.
   Here’s one solution: Library rules for America. Every time you walked into a school, a medical clinic, a drug store, a barbershop, an office, an airplane, a train, or a government building, you should see a sign that read:   or   or   . “In terms of the science, I am convinced that something like this library rule would reduce all modes of viral transmission,” Jimenez told me.
 Signs and guidance can only do so much. The backlash to masks among some Americans—mostly  conservative men—suggests that a certain segment of the population might rebel against library rules by, say, filming videos of themselves purposefully yelling into a barista’s face.
 “What we’ve learned from the mask experience is that many Americans don’t like being told what to do if they don’t understand why it’s necessary,” Jimenez said. “People need to understand that this virus is in the air, and that they breathe out 10 times more virus when they are shouting or speaking loudly.”
  Most Americans aren’t used to thinking about  any auditory dimension of this pandemic. For example, online shame-mongers often post pictures of ostensibly crowded beaches. These snapshots don’t capture several important factors, such as how long people are standing right next to one another. Crucially, they don’t tell us anything about sound. Are those sunbathers under a big umbrella shouting at each other about beach decorum, or quietly immersed in a book? In a plague spread commonly through the air, the  soundscape of our interactions is as important as the photo image of our behavior.
 A superior focus on exhalation science and aerosols might help people understand what environments are surprisingly safe, or surprisingly risky. In many cities, restaurants have recovered faster than movie theaters. But movie theaters with spaced-out seating, universal mask wearing, and complete silence might be less risky than indoor restaurants, where diners are talking and chewing without masks. “I can’t name a movie-theater outbreak in the contact-tracing literature,” Jimenez said, while also stressing that movie theaters cannot be considered risk-free, given that they are poorly ventilated indoor spaces where people spend several consecutive hours. “I can tell you that theaters don’t seem nearly as dangerous as a loud restaurant or bar, where people have to speak loudly to be heard.”
    Gyms, by contrast, might seem safe if everybody is working out in reverent silence. But  one study of exhalation flows by researchers at the University of Cassino and Southern Lazio in Italy estimated that most people expel more viral particles while breathing during heavy workouts than they do standing and speaking. Talking normally after a heavy workout could expel as many viral particles as shouting while standing at rest. These estimates are rough and differ from person to person. But the clear takeaway is that huffing-and-puffing on a stationary bike in a crowded room is risky, even if you never say a word.
 Exhalation mitigation can’t be considered in a vacuum. It’s part of a holistic approach to stamping out the virus while cautiously resuming the activities that bring joy, meaning, and community (and, yes, consumer revenue) to our neighborhoods and cities.
  The best way to keep people safe from COVID-19 to understand the full story of viral transmission and reverse engineer a barrier for every step in the process. Imagine, Kurt Vonnegut–style, a bullet of viral transmission traveling backwards in time out of its newly infected host. The virus-soaked aerosol dislodges from the patient’s lungs or nasal cavity. It magically travels out through their nose, into the air, where it lingers for several minutes, before being inhaled by a man, without a mask, who is yelling backward gibberish in a crowd.
 We can intervene at every stage in this story, with masks to block the virus, social distancing to separate the crowd, and ventilation to disperse the aerosols. But why would we ignore the founding event, which is the minting of the viral bullet? Spare your voice; save a life.

           is a staff writer at  The Atlantic, where he writes about economics, technology, and the media. He is the author of   Hit Makers and the host of the podcast   Crazy/Genius.
title: 戴上面具，闭上嘴

note_en:
The sale of TikTok's US operations to a domestic buyer — likely Microsoft or Oracle — has been thrown into disarray after China rewrote its technology export rules to give itself a veto over the process.
 According to The Wall Street Journal, negotiators for TikTok parent ByteDance and its main suitors were planning to advance their discussions over the weekend, but shelved those plans in light of the new rules.
 China's government on Friday issued  an update to its regulations governing how technology can be exported, adding 23 new categories of technology which require government approval before being sold.
     Chinese officials have not explicitly said that a TikTok sale would be affected under these regulations.
 However, both the Journal  and Bloomberg cited sources saying that the update to the tech export rules — which were last changed in 2008 — was made with the TikTok deal in mind.
 China's official Xinhua news agency said as much in  an article Sunday which cited a trade adviser to the government who said ByteDance "will need to comply" with the new rules.
 ByteDance issued a statement Sunday saying that it was aware of the new rules and would follow them.
   China's intervention added a new layer of complexity to the TikTok sale, prompted by a pending US ban on its operation contained an in executive order by President Donald Trump.
 TikTok is suing the Trump administration to block the ban, while negotiating with potential buyers at the same time.
 Analysts and commentators have suggested that TikTok's US operations could sell for more than $20 billion.

作为澳大利亚墨尔本一家大型公共创伤医院的医学物理学家，我不是一线工作人员，但我的很多放射科同事都是与新冠肺炎阳性或疑似患者面对面的。随着工作人员变得更加疲惫，面对大流行的许多挑战，我想通过让他们的工作更安全、更容易来支持他们。
我们听说了一种通过玻璃拍摄胸部X光的巧妙技术。从理论上讲，这允许X光机留在隔离患者的房间外-保存个人防护设备(PPE)，降低了放射技师的风险，并加快了这一过程。
我们首先在推特上看到了这项技术的细节。此外，2016年一篇文章的作者解释了埃博拉病毒的成像和感染控制方面(光谱2016-23-18)，这似乎同样适用于新冠肺炎。
第一步是让我们的工作人员相信这是一种安全和合理的胸部X光拍摄方法，但要找到解释这项技术的辐射安全或图像质量方面的出版物并不容易。这促使我们回答有关X射线透过玻璃的大小、散射辐射造成的安全问题以及最有用的技术参数等问题。
我们最近在“医学中的物理和工程科学”上发表了我们的研究结果。我们发现110kV和5.5 mAs是最常用的考虑玻璃和到患者的额外距离的因素(X线管和探测器之间的距离可以达到3m)。玻璃通常相当于约半个值层(即它将X射线束的强度降低了约50%，并且还提高了X射线束的光束质量或平均能量)。
我们的放射科医生对图像质量很满意，90%的通过玻璃拍摄的图像被认为是诊断性的。在我们的参数下，无论X射线是否透过玻璃，对患者的典型辐射剂量都是相同的。
这项技术已经被确定用于目前的新冠肺炎大流行，我们相应地进行了辐射安全性评估。放射技师和/或护士不需要穿铅质围裙。这一点很重要，因为他们已经佩戴了个人防护用品的数量，以及与共享铅质围裙相关的感染控制风险。
透过玻璃拍摄胸部X光时，站在1米外的工作人员所受的辐射剂量，相当于澳洲约3小时的天然本底辐射(低於0.5微西弗)，更远的地方则更低。我们鼓励工作人员最大限度地远离被成像的病人，也尽量远离房间外玻璃的后向散射。
我们现在已经用这种技术做了几百次胸部X光检查。在重症监护病房(ICU)查房中，一名放射技师和一名护士往往呆在房间里，而第二名放射技师在房间外操作X光机。在急诊科，房间没有那么大，工作人员在做X光检查时离开房间。这项技术绝对不能通过铅玻璃使用。
工作人员需要考虑这项技术是否适用于每个患者，以及临床问题。例如，在体型较大的患者半直立的情况下，确认宽孔鼻胃管的位置是没有用的。在这些情况下，仰卧式室内技术将更快地回答临床问题，并且需要重复X光的可能性较小。
实施这项技术的亮点之一是展示了团队合作和协作精神。放射科医生、放射摄影师、物理学家、感染预防护士、ICU护士和研究人员都在一起工作。适应性是我们应对大流行的关键。
这项技术加强了我们对新冠肺炎的防御。我们所有人都已经耗尽了资源来应对这场流行病，我们希望我们的“如何”指南能为其他人提供足够的信息，以便将“胸部X光透视玻璃”技术付诸实践。
顺便说一句，如果你对这篇文章标题第一部分的来源感到好奇，那就是来自冷切尔的歌曲《站在外面》，这首歌出现在这支澳大利亚乐队1980年的专辑《East》中。

note_en:
As a medical physicist at a large public trauma hospital in Melbourne, Australia, I’m not a frontline worker, but many of my colleagues in radiology are face-to-face with COVID-19 positive or suspected patients. As staff get more fatigued and confront the many challenges of the pandemic, I wanted to support them by making their work safer and easier.
 We heard about a clever technique for taking chest X-rays through glass. In theory, this allows the X-ray unit to remain outside an isolated patient’s room – conserving personal protective equipment (PPE), reducing radiographer risk and speeding up the process.
   We first saw details about the technique on Twitter. Also, authors of a 2016 article have explained about imaging and the infection-control aspects for the Ebola virus (  Spectrum 2016  23 18), which seemed equally applicable to COVID-19.
 The first step was to convince our staff this was a safe and reasonable way to take chest X-rays, but it was not easy to find publications that explained the radiation-safety or image-quality aspects of the technique. This motivated us to answer questions about the magnitude of X-ray transmission through glass, safety aspects caused by scattered radiation and the technique parameters that would be most useful.
  We’ve published our findings recently in   Physical and Engineering Sciences in Medicine. We found that 110 kV and 5.5 mAs were the most commonly used factors to account for the glass and additional distance to the patient (this could be up to 3 m between the X-ray tube and detector). The glass was typically equivalent to about one half-value-layer (i.e. it reduced the intensity of the X-ray beam by about 50% and also increased the beam quality or average energy of the X-ray beam).
 Our radiologists were satisfied with the image quality, with 90% of images taken through glass considered diagnostic. With our parameters, the typical radiation dose to patients is the same whether the X-ray is taken through glass or not.
 The technique has been established for use in the current COVID-19 pandemic, and we assessed the radiation safety accordingly. The radiographers and/or nurses do not need to wear a lead apron. This was important because of the amount of PPE they were already wearing and the infection-control risks associated with sharing lead aprons.
   The radiation dose to staff standing 1 m away while a chest X-ray is taken through glass is equivalent to approximately three hours of natural background radiation in Australia (less than 0.5 µSv) and is even lower further away. We encourage staff to maximize their distance from the patient being imaged and also from the backscatter off the glass outside the room.
 We have now performed several hundred chest X-rays using this technique. In intensive care unit (ICU) rounds, one radiographer and a nurse tend to stay in the room, while the second radiographer operates the X-ray unit from outside the room. In the emergency department, where the rooms are not as large, staff leave the room while the X-ray is performed. This technique should never be used through lead glass.
 Staff need to consider whether the technique is appropriate for each individual patient and also the clinical question. For example, it is not going to be useful where a large patient is semi-erect to confirm the wide-bore nasogastric tube position. In these cases, a supine in-room technique will answer the clinical question more quickly and with less chance of needing to repeat the X-ray.
  One of the highlights of implementing this technique was the teamwork and collaborative spirit displayed. Radiologists, radiographers, physicists, infection-prevention nurses, ICU nurses and researchers all worked together. Adaptability is key in our response to the pandemic.
 This technique strengthens our defence against COVID‑19. All of us have stretched resources coping with the pandemic and we hope our “how to” guide provides enough information for others to put the “chest X-ray through glass” technique into practice.
 By the way, in case you are curious about the origin of the first part of the headline for this article, it comes from Cold Chisel’s song “Standing on the outside”, which appears in the Australian band’s 1980 album called  East.

title: 站在外面看里面：透过玻璃的X射线
title_en: Standing on the outside looking in: X-rays through glass

众议院共和党领袖史蒂夫·斯卡利斯(Steve Scalise)删除了一段推文视频，视频中包含民主党总统候选人乔·拜登(Joe Biden)和活动家阿迪·巴坎(Ady Barkan)之间经过编辑的对话。修改后的视频似乎显示，拜登明确支持减少对警察的拨款，而真正的剪辑则更加模棱两可。
这段视频是一段较长视频的一部分，该视频将民主党人描绘成废除警察和暴徒统治的倡导者。上周末，Twitter给这条推文贴上了一个被操纵的媒体的标签。
患有肌萎缩侧索硬化症的巴尔坎写道，这些不是我说的话，因此他说话时带有一种人工合成的电脑声音。我已经失去了说话的能力，但我的机构和我的思想都没有。&#34；他呼吁路易斯安那州的众议院共和党党鞭斯卡利斯(Scalise)删除这段视频。他说，你应该向整个残疾人社区道歉。
斯卡利斯尊重了巴坎要求删除这条推文的要求，但他没有道歉。他重新发布了视频的一个版本，删除了篡改的部分，并为自己关于拜登想要削减警方资金的说法进行了辩护。
斯卡利斯写道，在一次采访中，拜登两次回答了关于重新分配警方资金的问题，肯定回答了。民主党和他们在媒体中的合作伙伴想指责编辑，假装这并不完全是他所相信的。
这是一个敏感的问题，因为拜登拒绝了许多他自己的支持者要求撤资警察的呼吁。拜登呼吁增加社会服务的资金，但没有明确支持大幅削减警察预算-这一想法在左翼人士中很受欢迎，但在普通美国人中并不受欢迎。拜登在他的竞选网站上呼吁适度增加3亿美元的警察项目，该项目提供联邦资金来雇用当地警察。
Scalise视频中修改后的视频剪辑来自7月份巴尔坎和拜登之间的一次采访。巴尔坎向拜登建议，政策制定者可以减少分配给警察的职责，并将警察的部分资金重新用于社会服务。
你对这种改革持开放态度吗？&#34；巴坎问道。是的，拜登回答说，详细说明了他计划增加精神健康服务的资金，更好地收集警察不当行为的统计数据，以及停止向警察部门出售军事硬件。他尖锐地没有说任何关于削减警察资金的事情。
巴肯切断了拜登的电话：我们同意将部分资金转用吗？
拜登又一次回答说，是的，绝对是。然后他谈到了改革监狱系统的必要性，又一次没有提到削减警察经费。
拜登迅速转移话题，造成了一些含糊的问题，拜登是支持削减警察经费，还是回避巴坎在这个话题上的问题。
斯卡利斯的视频包括了巴尔坎采访拜登的最后一段，但有人在巴尔坎的问题中加入了警察一词，这样他似乎是在问拜登，我们是否同意将部分资金转给警察？
视频随后显示，拜登的回答绝对是肯定的，这给人的印象是，拜登毫不含糊地支持削减警察经费。
拜登周日在推特上写道，这段视频是经过篡改的--这是一种公然散布错误信息的行为，目的是以一名使用辅助技术的男子为代价来传播错误信息。&#34；现在应该将其移除。&#34；
title: 共和党高层删除带有篡改拜登视频片段的推文
title_en: Top Republican deletes tweet with doctored Biden video footage

私人汽车已经作为自由的工程表达卖给了社会，通过各种各样的故事讲述了这一点，比如愤怒的葡萄(Grapes Of Wrath)的沙尘暴迁移到不负责任的炮弹长跑(Cannonball Run)的公路赛车手。而且，几十年来对快速流动的液态碳氢化合物燃料的依赖，使得人们几乎不能容忍花费更多的时间插电和静止，等待锂离子电池充电。因此，当谈到板凳赛车时，大多数人唯一关心的统计数据是，在你需要再次插入电源之前，它能走多远。
监管机构使用的测试对这种情况没有确切的帮助。在欧洲，WLTP测试周期平均为每小时29英里(47公里/小时)，产生的距离估计应该被认为是北美道路上的纯粹幻想。美国环境保护署的测试平均水平几乎是这个数字的两倍，但随后受到了一种欺骗因素的影响，一些人受到重罚，另一些人则受到奉承。这就是为什么看到几辆电动汽车的独立行驶里程测试结果很有趣的原因，该测试包括给电动汽车充电，然后以稳定的每小时70英里(112公里/小时)的速度驾驶，直到它们停下来。
这项研究是由北极星委托进行的，该公司希望将其新款北极星2电动汽车与三个竞争对手相抗衡：特斯拉Model 3性能、捷豹i-Pace和奥迪e-tron。测试程序于7月28日在密歇根州福勒维尔试验场的一个3英里(4.8公里)长的椭圆形上进行，非常简单。
每辆车都进入100%的充电状态，并在关闭再生制动的情况下设置为默认驾驶模式(或在最温和的设置下)。车内温度设置为72˚F(22˚C)，轮胎充气至每辆车车门牌子上建议的压力，并打开了前灯(这是测试赛道的要求)。
然后，这些汽车被缓慢行驶(时速低于25英里，时速1.3英里/40公里，时速2公里)从集结和充电区到椭圆形，然后每辆车都被轻轻加速(以0.3g)到GPS验证的每小时70英里，并保持在巡航控制系统下，所有的行驶都在测试赛道的同一车道上隔开。每辆车都被驾驶，直到由于电池耗尽而无法再保持每小时70英里的速度。
特斯拉Model 3行驶最远，达到234英里(377公里)-是EPA续航里程的75%-然后降至每小时70英里以下，这一点也就不足为奇了。特斯拉花了十多年的时间不断提高其电动动力总成的续航里程效率，并将车辆包裹在低阻力车身中，这种丰富的经验在测试赛道上显示出来，即使选择了20英寸的车轮和高性能轮胎。
北极星2号位居亚军，在时速70英里之前行驶了205英里(330公里)。北极星仍在等待EPA对北极星2的官方续航里程估计，但如果达到预期的250英里(402公里)，那将是该车官方续航里程的82%。
事实上，该公司为这次测试提供了一对北极星2，其中一辆配备了性能包(就像我们几周前测试的那辆车一样)的更大的20英寸车轮和粘稠的夏季轮胎。大轮子和粘性轮胎是你最不想要的汽车上的东西，因为要勉强维持每电子英里的里程，这种惩罚表现为与使用19英寸车轮和低滚动阻力橡胶的同一辆车相比，里程减少了8英里(13公里)，约为3%。
接下来是i-PACE，一款EV400HSE装饰鞋，有22英寸的轮子。这辆英国电池电动汽车在哭叔叔之前完成了188英里(302公里)，是EPA额定里程234英里(377公里)的80%。(捷豹最近发布了i-PACE的软件更新，将续航效率提高了12英里(19公里)，但这并没有影响Bev&39；EPA的官方续航里程。)。
排在最后一位的是奥迪e-tron，在结束一天的比赛之前，它已经跑了187英里(300公里)。然而，对奥迪的工程师来说，好的一面是，e-tron使其比其他任何一款车型都更接近EPA的行驶范围，行驶了官方额定204英里(328公里)的92%。奥迪也是第一个完全停下来的。其他每辆车都愉快地又行驶了几英里，离开椭圆形，回到一个插头上，即使仪表盘上指示的是0英里；e-tron在1.9英里(3公里)后停了下来。
一如既往，如果你需要一辆电动车来进行紧急的从东海岸到西海岸的送货，你仍然比不上特斯拉。
note_en:
The personal car has been sold to society as an engineered expression of freedom, as told through stories as varied as the dust bowl migration of  The Grapes of Wrath to the irresponsible road racers of  The Cannonball Run. And decades of dependence on fast-flowing liquid hydrocarbons for fuel has left little tolerance for spending many minutes more plugged in and stationary, waiting for lithium-ion cells to recharge. So when it comes to bench-racing electric vehicles, the only statistic most people care about is how far it can go before you need to plug it in again.
 The situation isn't exactly helped by the tests used by regulators. In Europe, the WLTP test cycle averages 29mph (47km/h) and generates range estimates that should be considered mere fantasies on North American roads. The  US Environmental Protection Agency's test averages almost twice that, but  then gets subjected to a fudge factor that  heavily penalizes some while  flattering others. Which is why it's interesting to see the results of an independent range test of several EVs that involved charging them up then driving them at a steady 70mph (112km/h) until they ground to a halt.
 The study was commissioned by Polestar, which wanted to rank its new  Polestar 2 EV against three competitors: the  Tesla Model 3 Performance,  Jaguar I-Pace, and  Audi e-tron. The test procedure, conducted on July 28 on a three-mile (4.8km) oval at Fowlerville Proving Ground in Michigan, was quite straightforward.
 Each car was brought to 100 percent state of charge, and set to its default drive mode with regenerative braking turned off (or in its most gentle setting). The interior temperature was set to 72˚F (22˚C), the tires were inflated to the pressures recommended on each car's door placard, and the headlights were turned on (a requirement of the test track).
 The cars were then driven slowly (under 25mph for 1.3 miles/40km/h for 2km) from the staging and charging area to the oval whereupon each was gently accelerated (at 0.3G) to a GPS-verified 70mph, and kept there with cruise control, all running spaced out in the same lane at the test track. Each car was driven until it could no longer maintain a speed of 70mph due to battery depletion.
  It should not come as a surprise that the Tesla Model 3 travelled furthest, reaching 234 miles (377km)—75 percent of its EPA range—before dropping below 70mph. Tesla has spent more than a decade continuously refining the range efficiency of its electric powertrains and clads its vehicles in low-drag bodies, and that wealth of experience showed on the test track, even when optioned with 20-inch wheels and high performance tires.
 The Polestar 2 was the runner up, traveling 205 miles (330km) before 70mph was too much. Polestar is still waiting on the official EPA range estimate for the Polestar 2, but if that comes in at the expected 250 miles (402km), that would be 82 percent of the car's official range.
 In fact, the company supplied a pair of Polestar 2s for the test, one of them equipped (like the car we tested a few weeks ago) with the Performance Package's bigger 20-inch wheels and sticky summer tires. Big wheels and sticky tires are the last thing you want on a car when it comes to eking out more miles per electron, and that penalty was on display as a range reduction of eight miles (13km), or about three percent, compared to the same car on 19-inch wheels and low-rolling resistance rubber.
 Next was the I-Pace, an EV400 HSE trim shod with 22-inch wheels. The British battery EV completed 188 miles (302km) before crying Uncle, 80 percent of its EPA-rated range of 234 miles (377km). ( Jaguar recently sent out a software update for the I-Pace that boosted range efficiency by up to 12 miles (19km), but that has not affected  the BEV's official EPA range.)
 In last place was the Audi e-tron, which managed 187 miles (300km) before calling it a day. However, the bright side for Audi's engineers is that the e-tron made it closer to its EPA range than any of the others, traveling 92 percent of the officially rated 204 miles (328km). The Audi was also the first to come to a complete stop. Each of the other cars happily traveled another couple of miles to exit the oval and return to a plug, even with an indicated 0 miles on the dash; the e-tron ground to a halt after 1.9 miles (3km).
 As continues to be the case, if you're in need of an electric car to make urgent coast-to-coast deliveries, you still can't beat a Tesla.

title_en: Tesla’s Model 3 beats competitors in 70mph range test
